executive_summary.one_paragraph: SRC is canonically an intracellular, N-myristoylated kinase, but in cancer cells it undergoes topological inversion via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE), exposing its kinase domain on the extracellular face of the plasma membrane as eSrc. This cancer-selective surface form is directly supported by live-cell functional assays, surface biotinylation (U251 MG; WB+MS dual readout), and in vivo xenograft antibody-killing studies. Surface accessibility is thus high in cancer but essentially absent in normal cells, making state dependence high and creating a favorable cancer-selective targeting window.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: Surface expression of SRC (eSrc) in cancer is supported by multiple direct, non-permeabilizing methods from independent sources. PMID:41818370 provides live-cell functional assays demonstrating topological inversion and extracellular accessibility of Src via autophagolysosomal exocytosis, including antibody-mediated tumor killing in cell culture and mouse xenograft models — consistent with live-cell surface exposure. PMID:41818382 (independent companion paper) independently confirms exocytosis-mediated Src surface exposure. Additionally, surface biotinylation on live U251 MG glioblastoma cells (PMC:PMC12749419) with dual WB+MS readout is documented as a direct, non-permeabilizing surface assay. One IHC report (PMC:PMC12012843) scores SRC as cytoplasmic rather than membranous in fixed tissue — a mild tension, but this reflects standard permeabilized IHC of bulk tissue rather than directly contradicting the cancer-specific surface inversion mechanism. Overall, ≥2 direct method types (live-cell functional/antibody engagement and surface biotinylation) from independent sources support surface accessibility.

antibodies.name: anti-SRC
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Scored for cytoplasmic staining intensity only; IHC protocol does not support plasma-membrane localization of SRC.

expression_observations.context: Clinical tumor tissue sections (human); SRC scored cytoplasmic staining 0-3 (negative/weak/moderate/strong); membranous staining not evaluated for SRC
U251 MG glioblastoma cells; sulfo-NHS biotin labeling of live-cell surface proteins followed by streptavidin enrichment and Western blot + mass spectrometry dual readout

antibodies.name: anti-SRC (inferred; indicated antibodies used on streptavidin-agarose eluate)

expression_observations.context: PDX cancer model xenografts (multiple cancer types); >90% of detected N-glycoproteins predicted to have surface membrane localization and/or signal peptide, validating method specificity

SKBR3 HER2+ breast cancer cell line; 1316 surface proteins identified by mass spectrometry surfaceome profiling; proteins categorized into receptors/catalytic, transporters, adhesion/junction, and immune CDs

antibodies.name: anti-ZNRF3 (HA-tagged; used to monitor ZNRF3 surface accessibility as validation target)

expression_observations.context: H1703 lung cancer cell line with TetOn-inducible ZNRF3-HA; surface biotinylation to test surface protein predictions

antibodies.name: anti-NIS (used as PM fraction vs WCL readout target)
surface marker antibody panel (murine bone marrow-derived monocytes, T cells, bEnd.3 endothelial cells)

expression_observations.context: Murine bone marrow-derived monocytes, T cells, and bEnd.3 endothelial cells; live-cell non-permeabilizing surface staining
MDA-MB-231 triple-negative breast cancer cell line; live-cell flow cytometry for non-canonical surface proteins csGRP78 and csCD44v
U251 MG-GCPII glioblastoma cell line (GCPII overexpressing); proximity biotinylation on intact live cells using novel iBody detection reagents

antibodies.name: anti-endoglin (used for IP after surface biotinylation)

expression_observations.context: Control and Eng-/- mouse embryonic endothelial cells (MEECs); cell surface biotinylation followed by endoglin immunoprecipitation to assess surface endoglin/IR interactions

antibodies.name: anti-Src (surface-accessible epitope; used for antibody-based therapeutic targeting of eSrc)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Antibody accesses extracellular domain of noncanonically surface-inverted Src; validated by functional tumor cell killing in vitro and in xenograft models.

expression_observations.context: Cancer cell lines (in vitro): Src noncanonically translocated and inverted onto outer cell surface via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE); eSrc detected at cell surface
Mouse xenograft tumor models: extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) found in primary tumors; anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediate tumor cell killing in xenograft models
Cancer cells (secondary source/commentary): Src exposed at outer surface via exocytosis, poised for therapeutic targeting

non_surface_expression.context: Human tumor tissue (clinical sections); SRC scored for cytoplasmic staining intensity (not membranous) using permeabilized IHC on fixed tissue

therapeutic_engagement.description: Anti-Src antibody-based therapies targeting extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) have demonstrated tumor cell killing in cell culture and in mouse xenograft models (academic preclinical proof-of-concept; PMID:41818370). No named clinical-stage drug or sponsor beyond academic research is documented in this ledger. A companion commentary (PMID:41818382) independently confirms the therapeutic targeting rationale.
therapeutic_engagement.surface_form_rationale: SRC is canonically an intracellular, N-myristoylated kinase. In cancer cells it undergoes topological inversion via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE), exposing its kinase domain on the extracellular face of the plasma membrane. Anti-Src antibody therapeutics engage this surface-inverted, membrane-anchored form (eSrc). No secreted soluble form of Src is documented in this ledger.

dual_localization.condition: normal (non-cancer) cells — canonical intracellular N-myristoylated state
cancer cells — topological inversion via autophagolysosomal exocytosis

anatomical_accessibility.rationale: HPA data places SRC at cell junctions in epithelial tissues. Junction-restricted localization is shielded from systemic circulation by tight junctions, limiting access for intravenously delivered binders.

anatomical_accessibility.context: cancer cells (solid tumors, in vitro and in vivo)
anatomical_accessibility.rationale: In cancer cells, SRC is noncanonically translocated via autophagolysosomal exocytosis to the outer plasma membrane (eSrc), exposing it to the extracellular/interstitial space. Antibody-based therapies reach eSrc in xenograft models, confirming systemic accessibility in the tumor microenvironment.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal (non-cancer) cells where SRC is an intracellular N-myristoylated kinase anchored to the cytoplasmic face of membranes
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cancer cells (in vitro cell lines and primary tumors in vivo) undergoing oncogenic transformation
accessibility_modulation.change: SRC undergoes topological inversion and noncanonical translocation onto the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane, becoming extracellular membrane-associated eSrc. This surface form is absent in normal cells.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: eSrc is exposed at the extracellular surface exclusively in cancer, making the kinase domain accessible to therapeutic antibodies and other extracellular binders specifically in tumor cells, creating a cancer-selective targeting opportunity.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal cells lacking active autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) directed at surface delivery of SRC
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cancer cell lines with prominent autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) activity
accessibility_modulation.change: Autophagolysosomal exocytosis serves as the secretory mechanism delivering SRC to the outer cell surface, exposing the kinase at the extracellular face via fusion of autolysosomes with the plasma membrane.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface accessibility of SRC in cancer depends on active ALE; inhibiting this pathway could reduce eSrc surface levels. Conversely, high ALE activity in tumors ensures robust extracellular SRC exposure for antibody-based targeting.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: General cell context (HPA tissue panel, multiple cell types) where SRC is primarily an intracellular kinase associated with the inner leaflet of membranes and vesicles
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cells expressing surface-associated SRC (plasma membrane and cell junction pools coexisting with intracellular vesicular pool)
accessibility_modulation.change: SRC distributes across cell junctions, bulk plasma membrane, and intracellular vesicles simultaneously, indicating a split between PM-associated and vesicular/intracellular compartments.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Only the plasma membrane and junctional fractions are extracellularly accessible; the vesicular intracellular pool is not. Binder access is therefore partial and spatially restricted to junctional and bulk PM domains.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Early-stage chondrogenic micromass culture (day 1) prior to histodifferentiation
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Chondrogenic cells at progressive differentiation stages (days 3, 6, 10, 15) during embryonic chondrogenesis
accessibility_modulation.change: Temporal surfaceome profiling by sialoglycoprotein enrichment/LC-MS/MS places SRC in surfaceome cluster 2, with surface availability shifting across differentiation time points during chondrocyte histodifferentiation.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface-accessible SRC is present during chondrogenic differentiation but its abundance at the surface is gated by developmental stage; therapeutic or imaging access to surface SRC in cartilage would be stage-dependent.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Resting MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-like cells without fluid shear stress stimulation
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-like cells subjected to fluid shear stress, or expressing constitutively active c-Src (Y527F)
accessibility_modulation.change: Fluid shear stress increases c-Src Tyr416 phosphorylation (activation) and promotes redistribution of downstream effectors (RANKL) toward the cell periphery/membrane fraction; constitutively active Src recapitulates this without mechanical input.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Mechanical activation of c-Src enhances membrane-proximal redistribution of associated proteins in osteoblasts; c-Src activation state modulates the surface-accessible pool of membrane cargo, suggesting mechanosensitive regulation of surface SRC signaling complexes.

structure.source: AlphaFold DB (STUB — fetchers not yet built)

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: Surface delivery of SRC depends on active ALE in cancer cells; this is a cell-state trafficking dependency rather than a classical co-receptor requirement. Inhibition of ALE would reduce eSrc surface levels. In normal cells lacking active ALE, SRC does not reach the outer plasma membrane surface.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: HPA places SRC at cell junctions in normal epithelial tissues. Junctional localization in normal cells is shielded from systemic circulation. In cancer, ALE-driven eSrc is distributed at the outer plasma membrane broadly, mitigating this risk for the therapeutic target context, but normal-tissue junctional SRC remains a concern for on-target/off-tumor access.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: SRC is a cytoplasmic kinase with no canonical extracellular domain or signal peptide. As eSrc, the topologically inverted kinase domain is membrane-anchored via the N-myristoyl anchor, presenting intracellular structural domains to the extracellular space. The accessible surface area is limited relative to type I transmembrane proteins with dedicated ECDs.

epitope_masking.rationale: The extracellular-facing domain of eSrc is the normally intracellular kinase domain, which adopts conformations evolved for cytoplasmic function. Epitope accessibility for exogenous antibodies is not well characterized for this inverted topology; the kinase domain may adopt conformations that partially occlude accessible epitopes or require specific activation states.

evidence.claim: Primary discovery claim: Src protein is noncanonically translocated and topologically inverted onto the outer cell surface in cancer cells, demonstrated both in vitro (cell lines) and in vivo (tumor models). This is a novel surface-expression finding for an otherwise classically intracellular kinase.

spans.quote: In this work, we found that Src is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer, both in vitro and in vivo.

evidence.claim: Topology claim: Src, which is N-myristoylated at Gly2 and normally faces the cytoplasmic leaflet of the plasma membrane, is topologically inverted in cancer such that its kinase domain is exposed on the extracellular face. This inverted orientation is targetable by antibody therapeutics, establishing an unconventional extracellular topology for Src in cancer.

spans.quote: Thus, intracellular N-myristoylated proteins, prototypically Src, can be topologically inverted onto the cell surface in cancer and targeted with antibody therapeutics.

evidence.claim: Mechanistic/methodological context: Src is delivered to the outer cell surface via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE), a secretory pathway prominent in cancer cell lines. This unconventional secretory route bypasses the classical ER-Golgi pathway and explains how a lipid-anchored, normally cytoplasmic kinase reaches the extracellular face of the plasma membrane.

spans.quote: We identified autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) as a secretory mechanism prominent in cancer cell lines.

evidence.claim: Therapeutic engagement: Extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) is found in primary tumors and is targetable by anti-Src antibody-based therapies that mediate tumor cell killing in cell culture and in mouse xenograft models. This demonstrates that the extracellular face of surface-inverted Src is accessible to antibody therapeutics in preclinical cancer models. Sponsor/stage: academic preclinical proof-of-concept.

spans.quote: Furthermore, this extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was found in primary tumors, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models.

evidence.claim: Independent corroborating surface-expression claim (companion paper/commentary): Src is exposed at the outer surface of cancer cells via exocytosis, positioned for therapeutic targeting. This secondary source confirms the surface-Src finding and its therapeutic implications from a separate publication.

spans.quote: Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting.

evidence.claim: IHC scoring rubric for SRC in clinical tissue sections: SRC was scored for cytoplasmic staining intensity (not membranous), graded 0–3 (negative/weak/moderate/strong). This methodological detail establishes that in standard IHC practice, SRC is detected in the cytoplasmic compartment — not the membrane compartment — qualifying the surface-expression interpretation. Pairs with surface-expression claims by indicating that conventional IHC does not support plasma-membrane localization of SRC.

spans.quote: AXL, SRC, and c‐MET staining was scored in a four‐tiered scoring system, evaluating membranous staining intensity for AXL and c‐MET, and cytoplasmic staining intensity for SRC as negative (score = 0), weak (score = 1), moderate (score = 2), or strong (score = 3).

evidence.claim: Cell-surface biotinylation method applied to U251 MG glioblastoma cells: following sulfo-NHS biotin labeling of live-cell surface proteins, lysates were enriched on streptavidin beads and analyzed by Western blot. This is a surface-biotinylation assay (non-permeabilizing, intact cells) in the context of surface proteome detection for GCPII-expressing cells — methodology documentation for paired WB step.

spans.quote: Following cell lysis and protein enrichment on streptavidin beads, we performed Western blot analysis of the resulting enriched proteins.

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation protocol detail: streptavidin-bead enriched eluants from U251 MG cells were analyzed by both Western blotting and mass spectrometry, establishing dual-readout validation for surface protein detection. Method_family: surface_biotinylation; permeabilization: none (intact live cells); readouts: WB + MS.

spans.quote: Protein eluants after streptavidin-bead enrichment were analyzed by Western blotting and mass spectrometry.

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation method protocol: biotinylated proteins isolated via streptavidin-agarose pulldown were subjected to Western blot analysis with specific antibodies. This describes the canonical sulfo-NHS biotinylation + streptavidin capture + WB readout pipeline used to detect surface proteins — methodology anchor for paired surface-expression results.

spans.quote: Biotinylated proteins were isolated using streptavidin-agarose and subjected to Western blot analysis using the indicated antibodies.

evidence.claim: Methodological validation: in N-glycoproteomic surface enrichment studies, over 90% of detected N-glycoproteins have predicted surface membrane localization and/or contain a signal peptide, underscoring the specificity of N-glycoproteomics for cell surface and secreted protein enrichment. This supports the reliability of MS-based surface capture methods for surface-expression annotation.

spans.quote: Over 90% of detected N -glycoproteins were predicted to have a surface membrane localization and/or contain a signal peptide ( Figure 1 B), 10 underscoring the specificity of N -glycoproteomics for cell surface and secreted protein enrichment.

evidence.claim: Cell surface capture (CSC) method detail: glycoproteins on intact live cells are oxidized at sialic acid residues to generate aldehydes, which react with hydrazide beads (stable hydrazone linkages), then captured glycoproteins are washed and released by PNGase F (N-glycan cleavage). This is the canonical Wollscheid/Aebersold CSC method for surfaceome MS profiling — method_family: mass_spec_surfaceome; permeabilization: none.

spans.quote: The aldehydes readily react with hydrazide, to form stable hydrazone linkages. 75 The enrichment technique called cell surface capture (CSC) developed by Wollscheid, Aebersold, and co-workers, utilized this chemistry to capture cell surface proteins onto hydrazide beads. 76 The captured glycoproteins were washed extensively and released using PNGase F, an amidase that cleaves between the innermost GlcNAc and the N -glycan-modified asparagine.

evidence.claim: Surfaceome characterization of SKBR3 breast cancer cells by mass spectrometry: 1316 surface proteins were identified and categorized into functional groups (receptors/catalytic, transporters, adhesion/junction, immune CDs). This surface proteome dataset from cancer cells provides reference context for surface protein classification methods.

spans.quote: To gain a better insight into these processes, a functional characterization of the SKBR3 surfaceome was performed by categorizing the set of 1316 proteins into four major groups: receptors and proteins with catalytic activity, transporters, cell adhesion/junction proteins, and proteins with immune functions such as CDs.

evidence.claim: Cell-surface biotinylation assay methodology documentation: surface biotinylation performed to test surface protein predictions; assay monitors ZNRF3 cell-surface accessibility. Method_family: surface_biotinylation; non-permeabilizing conditions.

spans.quote: To test these predictions we performed cell surface biotinylation assays and monitored ZNRF3.

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation protocol detail: surface proteins labeled with biotin on intact cells, lysates pulled down with streptavidin beads, then subjected to immunoblot analysis. This is the canonical non-permeabilizing sulfo-NHS biotinylation + streptavidin pulldown + WB pipeline — method documentation for surface_biotinylation assay class.

spans.quote: After labeling surface proteins with biotin, lysates were pulled down with streptavidin beads and subjected to immunoblot analysis.

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation result methodology: biotin-labeled plasma membrane (PM) proteins captured with streptavidin beads; abundance of PM-associated protein vs whole cell lysate (WCL) quantified by Western blot. Establishes PM fraction vs WCL comparison as readout — method_family: surface_biotinylation; permeabilization: none (intact live cells).

spans.quote: Labeled proteins were captured with streptavidin beads (surface protein fraction—PM) and the abundance of PM-associated NIS compared to whole cell lysates (WCL) was analyzed by Western blot (WB).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry performed on intact live murine cells (bone marrow-derived monocytes, T cells, bEnd.3 endothelial cells) to assess surface marker expression. Non-permeabilizing live-cell flow cytometry — method_family: flow_cytometry; fixation: live; permeabilization: none. Documents surface-marker detection methodology.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: bone marrow-derived monocytes, T cells, bEnd.3

spans.quote: Flow cytometry was performed to assess surface marker expression on murine bone marrow-derived monocytes, T cells, and bEnd.3 endothelial cells.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry on live MDA-MB-231 cells to detect cell-surface GRP78 (csGRP78) and csCD44v expression. Non-permeabilizing live-cell flow cytometry surface staining — method_family: flow_cytometry; fixation: live; permeabilization: none. Demonstrates surface-presence detection methodology applicable to non-canonical surface proteins.

spans.quote: ( b – d ) Flow cytometry analysis of csGRP78 and csCD44v expression on live MDA-MB-231 cells.

evidence.claim: Proximity biotinylation variant on intact live U251 MG-GCPII cells: Western blot detection of biotinylated surface proteins using HRP-, Ir-cat-, and RFT-conjugated iBodies. Method_family: surface_biotinylation (proximity variant); permeabilization: none; fixation: live. Documents alternative surface biotinylation approach with novel iBody detection reagents.

spans.quote: ( C-E ) Western blot analyses of proximity biotinylation on the U251 MG-GCPII cell surface using HRP , Ir cat , and RFT -conjugated iBodies.

evidence.claim: Cell-surface biotinylation assay performed on endothelial cells to detect surface protein accessibility; biotin-labeled proteins immunoprecipitated to assess cell-surface protein interactions. Method_family: surface_biotinylation; non-permeabilizing conditions on intact ECs.

spans.quote: To test this possibility, a cell surface biotinylation assay was performed on control and Eng−/− MEECs upon brief treatment with increasing concentrations of insulin prior to endoglin IP to assess for changes in cell surface endoglin/IR interactions ( Fig. 2 D ).

evidence.claim: HPA subcellular localization data places SRC protein at cell junctions, plasma membrane, and vesicles, supporting multi-compartment surface-associated distribution including junctional and bulk plasma membrane localization.

spans.quote: HPA subcellular locations: Cell Junctions;Plasma membrane;Vesicles.

evidence.claim: HPA flags SRC as junctional, indicating localization at cell-cell junctions and implying restricted anatomical accessibility at junction-adjacent plasma membrane domains.

spans.quote: HPA flags this gene as junctional.

evidence.claim: SRC is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer cells, both in vitro and in vivo, placing the kinase as an extracellular membrane-associated protein (eSrc) in cancer disease context. This represents a cancer-specific surface localization distinct from the canonical intracellular membrane-anchored state.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: cancer cell lines and primary tumors

spans.quote: In this work, we found that Src is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer, both in vitro and in vivo.

evidence.claim: Autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) is identified as the secretory mechanism by which SRC is delivered to the cell surface in cancer cells, representing an accessibility modulation pathway driven by lysosomal exocytosis in the cancer cell state.

spans.quote: We identified autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) as a secretory mechanism prominent in cancer cell lines.

evidence.claim: Extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) is detected in primary tumor tissue in vivo, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediate tumor cell killing in xenograft models, confirming that the cancer-state surface localization extends to primary human tumors. This establishes eSrc as a disease-state-induced surface antigen in solid cancer.

spans.quote: Furthermore, this extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was found in primary tumors, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models.

evidence.claim: Intracellular N-myristoylated SRC undergoes topological inversion onto the cell surface specifically in the cancer cell state, a modulation absent in normal cells. Baseline context: normal non-cancer cells, intracellular; modulating state: cancer transformation; change: surface-exposed eSrc; implication: SRC becomes accessible at the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane in cancer.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: cancer cell lines vs normal cells

spans.quote: Thus, intracellular N-myristoylated proteins, prototypically Src, can be topologically inverted onto the cell surface in cancer and targeted with antibody therapeutics.

evidence.claim: Exocytosis exposes SRC at the outer surface of cancer cells. This corroborates that eSrc surface localization is driven by an active secretory/exocytotic mechanism, and that the SRC extracellular domain is accessible for therapeutic antibody targeting specifically in cancer cells.

spans.quote: Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting.

evidence.claim: SRC kinase is detected in the surfaceome of chondrogenic cells (surfaceome cluster 2), where it participates in chondrocyte phenotype control. Surface-capture mass spectrometry evidence places SRC among cell-surface regulators of chondrocyte biology, including during chondrogenic differentiation.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: embryonic chondrogenic cells (chick limb bud micromass culture)

spans.quote: Surfaceome cluster 2 harbors several cell-surface regulators of chondrocyte biology, including RECK (a membrane-anchored matrix metalloprotease inhibitor [ 45 ]), FGFR2 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 2; regulates chondrocyte proliferation/differentiation signaling [ 46 ]), multiple ephrin receptors (EPHA7, EPHB2, EPHB1, EPHB3 with potential roles in cartilage morphogenesis [ 47 ]), SRC kinase (chondrocyte phenotype control [ 48 ]), and GPC1 (glypical-1

evidence.claim: SRC kinase appears in the surfaceome of chondrogenic cells with a role in chondrocyte phenotype control, consistent with surface-accessible SRC during cartilage development and regeneration. This complements the surfaceome cluster 2 finding and names the functional context explicitly.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: embryonic chondrogenic cells (chick limb bud micromass culture)

spans.quote: including RECK (a membrane-anchored matrix metalloprotease inhibitor [ 45 ]), FGFR2 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 2; regulates chondrocyte proliferation/differentiation signaling [ 46 ]), multiple ephrin receptors (EPHA7, EPHB2, EPHB1, EPHB3 with potential roles in cartilage morphogenesis [ 47 ]), SRC kinase (chondrocyte phenotype control [ 48 ]), and GPC1 (glypical-1; a proteoglycan and cell surface co-receptor involved in growth factor pathway modulation [ 49 ]), highlighting surfaceome mechanisms in cartilage development and regeneration.

evidence.claim: Temporal surfaceome profiling of chondrogenic cells across differentiation stages (days 1, 3, 6, 10, 15) using sialoglycoprotein enrichment with LC-MS/MS provides state-resolved surface-accessibility data, capturing how surface availability of SRC and other regulators shifts during histodifferentiation.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: chondrogenic micromass culture — staged differentiation days 1–15

spans.quote: Temporal proteomic profiling was conducted at days 1, 3, 6, 10, and 15 of micromass culture corresponding to key histodifferentiation stages [ 17 , 18 ], combining sialoglycoprotein enrichment via aminooxy-biotin conjugation with high-resolution LC-MS/MS on 3 biological replicates ( n = 3).

evidence.claim: In osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells, fluid shear stress activates c-Src (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promotes redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery with an increase of RANKL in the membrane fraction. This demonstrates that c-Src activation state is mechanosensitively regulated in osteoblasts, linking mechanical stimulation to c-Src-dependent membrane trafficking.

spans.quote: Fluid shear stress increased c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promoted redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery, accompanied by an increase of RANKL in the membrane fraction.

evidence.claim: Constitutively active c-Src (Y527F mutant) enhances peripheral localization of RANKL even in the absence of shear stress in osteoblast-like cells, indicating that c-Src activation level is a sufficient modulator of membrane-proximal localization independent of mechanical cues. This supports c-Src activity state as a key determinant of membrane accessibility in osteoblasts.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-like cells (constitutively active c-Src Y527F)

spans.quote: Moreover, constitutively active c-Src (Y527F) enhanced peripheral localization of RANKL even in the absence of shear stress.

evidence.claim: SRC (proto-oncogene tyrosine-protein kinase Src) is identified as one of four key regulatory proteins in a cytoplasmic actin-interaction subnetwork controlling NIS plasma-membrane trafficking in thyroid cells. SRC is among the top-scoring NIS-PM candidates, placing it as a regulator of surface retention in the thyroid/NIS context.

spans.quote: Further analysis with STRING software revealed that the 29 top-scoring NIS-PM candidates formed a tight interaction subnetwork around cytoplasmic actin (ACTB) and four of the regulatory proteins on the hsa04810 pathway ( Figure 3 A): the small GTPase RAC1 (RAS-related C3 botulinum toxin substrate 1), the proto-oncogene tyrosine-protein kinase Src (SRC), the actin-nucleating ARP 2/3 complex subunit 4 (ARPC4), and the actin-binding adaptor protein ezrin (EZRIN).

evidence.claim: The SRC/RAC1/PAK1/PIP5K/EZRIN pathway is established as a key regulator of NIS actin-cytoskeleton anchoring and retention at the plasma membrane in thyroid cells. This identifies SRC as a functional modulator of surface accessibility: baseline retention of NIS at the PM depends on SRC pathway activity.

spans.quote: Biochemical and functional analysis allowed us to establish the SRC/RAC1/PAK1/PIP5K/EZRIN pathway as a key regulator of NIS actin-cytoskeleton anchoring and retention at the PM [ 10 ].

confidence_reasoning: A1 grades evidence as direct_multi_method (live-cell functional assays + surface biotinylation + xenograft antibody engagement from independent sources, no contradicting evidence), which normally supports high confidence. Confidence is held at moderate because: (1) state dependence is high — eSrc surface form is exclusive to cancer cells via ALE, absent in normal cells, so the surface-accessible form is disease-state-conditional; (2) the ECD presented is the inverted kinase domain with minimal characterized extracellular surface, introducing uncertainty about binder accessibility; (3) A2 confirms junctional restriction in normal tissues as a background concern. The evidence base for the cancer-state surface form is strong, but the narrow biological window and non-canonical topology introduce meaningful translational uncertainty.
